question.title: @DateTimeFormat with daylight saving error when converting
question.body:
I am having a problem converting a date that actually does not exists using the @DateTimeFormat annotation.

For example, when I set the date 15/10/2017, with annotation in my entity being as follows:

@Column(nullable = false)
@NotNull
@DateTimeFormat(pattern = "dd/MM/yyyy")
private Date dataVisita;

I receive the error: 

Failed To Convert Property Value Of Type Java.Lang.String To Required Type Java.Util.Date For Property DataVisita; 
Nested Exception Is Org.Springframework.Core.Convert.ConversionFailedException: 
    Failed To Convert From Type Java.Lang.String To Type @Javax.Persistence.Column @Javax.Validation.Constraints.NotNull @Org.Springframework.Format.Annotation.DateTimeFormat Java.Util.Date For Value 15/10/2017; 
Nested Exception Is Java.Lang.IllegalArgumentException: 
    Cannot Parse "15/10/2017": Illegal Instant Due To Time Zone Offset Transition (America/Sao_Paulo)

I understand that the error tells me that the date 15/10/2017 00:00:00 does not actually exists, but I want to convert to 15/10/2017 01:00:00, ignoring this way the problem and finding the correspondent date.

Is there a way for me to override the @DateTimeFormat annotation or a way to point the formatter to be lenient?

answers.body:
Try this:

@Column(nullable = false)

@NotNull

@DateTimeFormat(pattern = "dd/MM/yyyy HH:mm:ss")

private Date dataVisita;

It seems to do what you want (adjust 0:00 to 1:00), you'll need to implement a validator as follows:

public class PersonValidator implements Validator {

    /**
     * This Validator validates *just* Person instances
     */
    public boolean supports(Class clazz) {
        return Date.class.equals(clazz);
    }

    public void validate(Object obj, Errors e) {
        String dateString = (String) obj;
        DateTimeFormatter fmt = DateTimeFormat.forPattern("dd/MM/yyyy HH:mm:ss");
        DateTime ourDate = null;
        try {
            fmt.parseDateTime(dateString);
        } catch (IllegalArgumentException e) {
            // massage your hour here
        } finally {
            ourDate = fmt.parseDateTime(dateString);
        }
    }
}

For me, you should not mess up directly with the locale of the system. That what your problem truely is.

I mean you didn't provide a time zone, so you are using the one of the server. On this server with its config, it will be this day/date. On another server it would another day/date.

Be sure that you work either in UTC (so no day light saving and all) or an explicit locale that the client provided in input, use a local date to store... Either keep it like that and when you need to compare dates, convert it to UTC. This way the issue would disapear.

A quick fix is to ensure your server use UTC but in the long run you should always control what locale you work with, so either the provided date has to be UTC or it is a Local date and another field provide the timezone.

If you want to use DateTimeFormat which is Java 8 class and part of new (well since java 8) "java.time" framework you probably need to use Java 8 Date classes:

DATE:
java.time.LocalDate

TIME:
java.time.LocalTime, java.time.OffsetTime

TIMESTAMP:
java.time.Instant, java.time.LocalDateTime, java.time.OffsetDateTime and java.time.ZonedDateTime

Also, which ORM are you using with your Spring framework? Spring data, hibernate or something else? If you use Hibernate, then consult the Hibernate User's guide: Mapping Date/Time Values

Depending on how you store your dates in DB, and your server timezone, the solution would be to set your default timezone to UTC on Spring initialisation, using the following code:

TimeZone.setDefault(TimeZone.getTimeZone("UTC"));

This should be really straightforward.

The issue here is , you are passing a String value for a Date Object. Here is the documentation for the annotation in question :

https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/docs/current/javadoc-api/org/springframework/format/annotation/DateTimeFormat.html

Simply put here are the types required: java.util.Date, java.util.Calendar, java.lang.Long, Joda-Time value types

A very naive way :

SimpleDateFormat sdf = new SimpleDateFormat("dd/MM/yyyy");
Date destnationDateToBeSaved = sdf.parse("15/10/2017");

Recommended way :

Use :

@DateTimeFormat(iso = ISO.DATE_TIME)
private ZonedDateTime zonedDateTime;

and

// 'yyyy-MMM-dd HH:mm:ss' pattern or if preferred ISO
ZonedDateTime  destnationDateToBeSaved= ZonedDateTime.parse("2017-Oct-15 23:35:05", DateTimeFormatter.ofPattern("yyyy-MMM-dd HH:mm:ss").withZone(ZoneId.of("UTC")));

Hope that helps !
